content.method: Systematic review and meta-analysis of existing compensatory-funding and teacher-incentive programs in comparable tracked systems; if no adequate evidence exists, design a randomized or stepped-wedge pilot in a subset of Hungarian feeder schools with compensatory funding, tracking gap closure over 3-5 years against non-pilot comparison schools.
content.question: Does compensatory funding, teacher-quality incentives, and instructional-time equalization (without altering track structure) produce measurable reductions in the documented SES/performance gap, or does the gap persist because selection and peer-sorting dynamics continue unabated?
content.why_it_matters: T4 is the lowest-friction, lowest-legal-risk proposal, but its entire rationale depends on whether non-structural levers can substantively close a 170-point reading gap; if the evidence shows this is implausible, T4 risks becoming a legitimizing gesture rather than substantive reform.
